messages.content: In the Hibernate library with an Animal interface (not partially abstract or concrete class) with concrete classes Cat and Dog, can the JOINED_TABLE strategy be used to get all the Cats and then all the Dogs individually but store them polymorphically at the Java, non-Hibernate level in a List<Animal>?

No, the JOINED_TABLE strategy cannot be used to achieve this specific requirement.

The JOINED_TABLE strategy creates separate tables for each concrete class in the inheritance hierarchy (in this case, Cat and Dog), and a separate table for the base class (Animal). Each table contains columns specific to its corresponding class, along with shared columns from the base class.

When querying the database using the JOINED_TABLE strategy, Hibernate will retrieve results specific to a particular concrete class. So, you can get all the Cats or all the Dogs individually with separate queries, but not both together in a single query.

To store the results polymorphically at the Java level in a `List<Animal>`, you would need to use a different inheritance mapping strategy that supports polymorphic queries, such as the TABLE_PER_CLASS or SINGLE_TABLE strategy.

With the TABLE_PER_CLASS strategy, each concrete class (Cat and Dog) will have its own table, and common attributes from the base class (Animal) are duplicated in each table. This approach allows querying and retrieving all instances of Animal, including Cats and Dogs, in a single query.

With the SINGLE_TABLE strategy, all classes in the inheritance hierarchy are mapped to a single table and differentiated using a discriminator column. This strategy also allows querying and retrieving all instances of Animal, including Cats and Dogs, in a single query.

So, to achieve the desired result of storing both Cats and Dogs polymorphically in a `List<Animal>`, you should consider using the TABLE_PER_CLASS or SINGLE_TABLE strategy instead of the JOINED_TABLE strategy.
I think you misunderstood what I said. I want Hibernate to give me only Cats and then only Dogs, but then in Java, I want to store those in a List<Animal>.
Can the returned Cats and Dogs be appended to the same List<Animal> immediately?
Give me the code again all together, but this time, make it so that Hibernate uses UUIDs.
Copy exactly what you just did, but any explanatory text should be multi-line comments (that is, using /* and */).
I meant all the explanatory parts that didn't use single-line comments (//) to use multi-line comments (/* and */).

Take the code that had single-line comments and don't change the single-line comments to multi-line ones. Only change the explanatory text with no commenting syntax so that it uses multi-line-comment syntax.

1) What does @Autowired do?

2) The /* and */ are shown to me as / and /, do what needs to be done for that to show properly to me.

3) If Animal is an interface, are the TABLE_PER_CLASS and JOINED strategies the same?

1) The `@Autowired` annotation is used in Spring Framework to automatically wire dependencies between beans. It allows the automatic resolution of beans by type from the Spring container, and the injection of those beans into the classes that need them. Basically, `@Autowired` ensures that the dependencies required by a class are automatically resolved and provided by the Spring container.

2) Apologies for the display issue with the /* and */ symbols. It seems that some symbols are not rendering correctly. Please ensure that you are viewing the code in a text editor or IDE that supports multi-line comments using /* and */ syntax.

3) No, the TABLE_PER_CLASS and JOINED inheritance strategies are not the same, even if the parent class is an interface like Animal.

- The TABLE_PER_CLASS strategy creates a separate table for each concrete class in the inheritance hierarchy, including the parent class (i.e., Animal in this case). Each table contains its specific fields, including the inherited fields from the parent class. Polymorphic queries can be used in this strategy to fetch all instances of Animal, including Cats and Dogs in a single query.

- On the other hand, the JOINED strategy, as the name suggests, joins the tables of the parent and child classes based on relationships defined in the database schema. In this strategy, each concrete class has its own table in the database, and the shared fields from the parent class are also stored in the parent table. Polymorphic queries can be used to retrieve instances of specific concrete classes (e.g., Cats or Dogs) individually.

So, even if Animal is an interface, the TABLE_PER_CLASS and JOINED strategies differ in terms of the database schema they create and the way they handle polymorphic queries.
3) Did you assume that Animal is an interface.

Apologies for the incorrect assumption. In your initial question, you mentioned that Animal is an interface, and based on that information, the response was provided. 

However, if Animal is not an interface but a superclass or a concrete class in the inheritance hierarchy, then the answer would be different. 

For a superclass or concrete class like Animal in the inheritance hierarchy, the TABLE_PER_CLASS and JOINED strategies would not be the same. The TABLE_PER_CLASS strategy creates a separate table for each concrete class (including Animal) in the hierarchy, while the JOINED strategy joins tables based on relationships defined in the schema, linking the parent and child tables together.

So, depending on whether Animal is an interface or a concrete class, the TABLE_PER_CLASS and JOINED strategies would behave differently.
I'm asking if Animal is in fact an interface, are the TABLE_PER_CLASS and JOINED strategies the same?

Apologies for the confusion. If Animal is an interface and not a concrete class or superclass, then the TABLE_PER_CLASS and JOINED strategies are essentially the same in the context of Hibernate inheritance mapping.

When using the TABLE_PER_CLASS strategy or the JOINED strategy with an interface, Hibernate will create separate tables for each concrete implementation of the interface. Each table will contain columns specific to its corresponding concrete class.

In both strategies, you can query for instances of a specific concrete class, such as Cats or Dogs, and retrieve them individually. However, you cannot directly query for all instances of the interface (Animal) itself, as interfaces do not have their own table in the database.

So, in summary, if Animal is an interface, the TABLE_PER_CLASS and JOINED strategies in Hibernate will behave similarly, creating separate tables for each concrete implementation of the interface and allowing individual querying of those concrete classes.

Assuming I want to query all Animals (and also that Animal is an interface, like before),

1) would it be possible to query all Animals with the TABLE_PER_CLASS strategy?

2) would it be possible to query all Animals with the JOINED strategy?

Apologies for the confusion in my previous response. If Animal is an interface, neither the TABLE_PER_CLASS strategy nor the JOINED strategy alone would allow you to directly query all Animals.

1) With the TABLE_PER_CLASS strategy
